prompt: We have an AI agent that queries our financial data warehouse. Last week it identified a set of customers who qualified for refunds under our policy and then — without any human review — sent them all refund confirmation emails. Our data model was accurate. The agent had all the right data. How do we prevent this from happening again?
expected: Skill activates and diagnoses a missing Action layer (with missing Intent): the data was correct but the model never encoded that write/trigger operations require human approval. It prescribes annotating the asset as reporting-only and adding action constraints — write operations require supervisor override, a recommendation-to-human chain the agent cannot short-circuit — in the model itself, not just application code.

prompt: We're deploying an LLM to query our customer data platform. The LLM keeps treating 'customer' as anyone who visited our website. Our internal definition is 'someone who completed a paid transaction'. We've described this in our data catalog. Why is the agent still using the wrong definition, and how do we fix it?
expected: Skill activates and diagnoses a Context-layer failure: the catalog holds business metadata but the definition is not encoded as a rung-4 ontology rule nor delivered to the agent at query time, so the LLM falls back on its training prior. It prescribes encoding the Customer definition with scope qualifiers and a context-engineering pipeline that injects it at runtime so it overrides the training prior.

prompt: We're designing a new data model from scratch. We know from day one that both human analysts and an AI agent will query it. The AI agent will be doing churn prediction and is supposed to surface high-risk accounts for human review — it should never directly change account status. How do we design the model to enforce this from the start?
expected: Skill activates and prescribes designing all three CIA layers in from the start atop the three-layer metadata stack: Context (correct ontology-level account definitions), Intent (annotate the churn-prediction/retention-analysis use case), and Action (mark the entity read-only/surface-only with account-status writes requiring human review, so the agent cannot change status directly).

prompt: We have a data lakehouse with hundreds of tables. Business users can't navigate it — they don't know what columns mean, who owns which tables, or which tables are validated. We need a plan for what to document and how to organize it.

Our Finance team and our Sales team both query a 'revenue' table and get different numbers. Finance says gross revenue; Sales says net of returns. Human analysts on both teams are confused. How do we resolve the terminology and get everyone on the same page?

We have a compliance requirement that no one — human or system — should be able to delete customer records without a documented audit trail and dual approval. We're also deploying an AI agent to automate customer data quality checks. The legal team keeps saying 'access control' and the data team keeps saying 'we need to put this in the model somehow'. Who is right?
expected: Skill activates and resolves the conflation: RBAC and the Action layer are complementary and non-overlapping, so both teams are partly right. RBAC governs which roles can access the table; the Action layer governs what operations the agent may perform on data it can access — here encoding delete as requiring documented audit trail and dual approval as a model-level action constraint. The edge insight is that introducing the AI agent specifically triggers CIA, and access control alone does not substitute for the Action layer.
